key_insight: Random solid-solution and local chemical order in WTaCrV change radiation response: they create more primary defects than an average-atom model, yet they better preserve mechanical strength under irradiation.
engineering_relevance: For nuclear structural materials, nanoscale chemical disorder can improve irradiation tolerance by limiting radiation-induced softening and maintaining flow stress.
method: Built three atomistic representations of WTaCrV: average-atom (AA), random solid-solution (RSS), and local chemical order (LCO).
Ran collision-cascade simulations with newly developed interatomic potentials to compare defect production and mechanical degradation.
Evaluated how point defects, lattice distortion, and deformation twins interact under irradiation.
evidence_signal: The simulations show Frenkel pair counts follow NRSS > NLCO > NAA at the same dose, while irradiation strongly degrades AA but has limited impact on strength and flow stress in RSS and LCO.
watch_item: Results depend on the fidelity of the new interatomic potentials and the idealized atomistic models, so transfer to real microstructures and irradiation conditions should be validated.
paper_title: Primary damage and mechanical degradation of WTaCrV refractory high-entropy alloy: effects of solid-solution and chemical ordering
